title: Listening - Part 3: Protecting the Environment
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about an environmental change they have made. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H what encouraged each speaker to make the change. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about an environmental change they have made. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H what encouraged each speaker to make the change. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about adopting a more environmentally friendly habit.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: I knew plastic waste was a problem, but it seemed distant from my own life, and I still bought drinks in disposable bottles most days. Then I watched a programme about plastic entering oceans and the effect on seabirds and fish. [19]Seeing those images made me realise that items I used for a few minutes could remain in the environment for years, so I began carrying a refillable bottle instead.[/19] It was an easy change once I accepted that my daily habits were part of the issue.||Speaker 2 Man: My family often spends Sundays walking through woods outside the town. One weekend we reached a picnic area covered with packaging and cans, some of it close to a stream. It completely spoiled a place I loved. [20]After seeing how much rubbish visitors had left behind in that beautiful area, I started taking a bag on walks and collecting litter whenever I find it.[/20] I can't clear the whole forest, but leaving a path cleaner than I found it seems worthwhile.||Speaker 3 Woman: I used to drive to the local shops even though the journey takes only five minutes. I told myself it was convenient, especially when it rained. My thirteen-year-old daughter had been learning about air pollution at school. [21]She kept asking why I used the car for such short distances and eventually persuaded me that we should walk or cycle whenever we only needed a few things.[/21] I was slightly embarrassed that she had to point it out, but I now enjoy those short journeys more.||Speaker 4 Man: Our college held a month-long competition between classes to see which group could reduce waste most. Points were awarded for bringing reusable lunch containers, repairing old objects and avoiding unnecessary printing. [22]I started printing notes on both sides and taking a reusable cup because I wanted my class to do well in the challenge.[/22] We didn’t win, but after a month the habits felt normal, so I continued them. Sometimes a little competition is enough to make you try something better.||Speaker 5 Woman: Our heating used to stay on even when nobody was at home, mostly because nobody could be bothered adjusting the controls. When energy prices rose, my father showed us how much we were paying each month. [23]We fitted better controls and agreed to wear warmer clothes before increasing the temperature, mainly because reducing our energy use would bring the bills down.[/23] Naturally, using less fuel is also better environmentally, but I must admit the cost was what first made us act.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: I knew plastic waste was a problem, but it seemed distant from my own life, and I still bought drinks in disposable bottles most days. Then I watched a programme about plastic entering oceans and the effect on seabirds and fish. Seeing those images made me realise that items I used for a few minutes could remain in the environment for years, so I began carrying a refillable bottle instead. It was an easy change once I accepted that my daily habits were part of the issue.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: My family often spends Sundays walking through woods outside the town. One weekend we reached a picnic area covered with packaging and cans, some of it close to a stream. It completely spoiled a place I loved. After seeing how much rubbish visitors had left behind in that beautiful area, I started taking a bag on walks and collecting litter whenever I find it. I can't clear the whole forest, but leaving a path cleaner than I found it seems worthwhile.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: I used to drive to the local shops even though the journey takes only five minutes. I told myself it was convenient, especially when it rained. My thirteen-year-old daughter had been learning about air pollution at school. She kept asking why I used the car for such short distances and eventually persuaded me that we should walk or cycle whenever we only needed a few things. I was slightly embarrassed that she had to point it out, but I now enjoy those short journeys more.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: Our college held a month-long competition between classes to see which group could reduce waste most. Points were awarded for bringing reusable lunch containers, repairing old objects and avoiding unnecessary printing. I started printing notes on both sides and taking a reusable cup because I wanted my class to do well in the challenge. We didn’t win, but after a month the habits felt normal, so I continued them. Sometimes a little competition is enough to make you try something better.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: Our heating used to stay on even when nobody was at home, mostly because nobody could be bothered adjusting the controls. When energy prices rose, my father showed us how much we were paying each month. We fitted better controls and agreed to wear warmer clothes before increasing the temperature, mainly because reducing our energy use would bring the bills down. Naturally, using less fuel is also better environmentally, but I must admit the cost was what first made us act.

options.text: seeing rubbish in a natural place
a challenge organised by their school
the influence of a younger relative

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: A documentary about ocean plastic motivated the speaker to stop buying disposable bottles.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The speaker began collecting litter after seeing rubbish in a favourite woodland area.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The speaker changed travel habits after being challenged by a younger family member.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: A college waste-reduction challenge prompted the speaker to adopt reusable items and reduce printing.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The household initially reduced heating use in order to lower energy costs.
